transcript:
What you're looking at is code — actual programming tokens like "function" and "const" and "parse" — scattered as a drifting field of text, occasionally cut through by cyan lines and swept by a pale bar of light moving left to right.

Here's the thing that changes everything: that sweeping bar isn't decoration, it's a reader. In the code, there's a variable literally called "sweepActive" that triggers a cascade — when text falls inside its path, a variable named "exhaustion" increases, and "warmth" — which starts around 0.3 to 0.7 — permanently drops. Look for the comment "permanent bleaching from repeated parsing." That's not reversible. There's even an "exhaustion floor" that only ever rises, meaning every pass leaves scar tissue the piece can never fully recover from.

So those cyan lines you keep seeing flicker into existence? Each one is the code's own definition of comprehension — literally named "boundaryRepulsion," the force of being segmented into meaning. The piece is describing, in real time, what happens when something reads it — and you're watching that violence accumulate, frame by frame, as permanent damage.
